verses.decision_notes: Narrator frame; YHWH resolves to our Father in narrator voice (FHB-1.2-1 Step 4, Genesis 1 anchor).

verses.fhb_text: "I know that you can do all things, and that no plan of yours can be thwarted.
verses.decision_notes: Job addresses God directly in prayer; second-person "you" preserved per speaker grammar (FHB-1.2-1 v4.3 — person addressing God).

lines.t: "I know that you can do all things
and that no plan of yours can be thwarted.

verses.fhb_text: You asked, 'Who is this that obscures my counsel without knowledge?' Surely I spoke of things I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.
verses.decision_notes: Job quotes back the Father's whirlwind question (38:2); "You asked" supplied for clarity that Job is citing the rebuke he received.

lines.t: You asked, 'Who is this that obscures my counsel without knowledge?' Surely I spoke of things I did not understand, things too wonderful for me, which I did not know.

verses.fhb_text: You said, 'Listen now, and I will speak; I will question you, and you must answer me.'
verses.decision_notes: Job again quotes the Father's words (38:3, 40:7) back to him in his confession.

verses.fhb_text: My ears had heard of you, but now my eyes have seen you.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father-Heart Revelation — the climax of the whole Job arc (FHB-2.9-1).

Renderings:
• "My ears had heard of you, but now my eyes have seen you" — Job moves from hearing about God in the friends' distant register to seeing the Father face to face

Hebrew/Greek: l'shema ozen / ayin ra'atcha (by the hearing of the ear / my eye has seen you) → preserved as the seeing-knowing breakthrough
verses.footnote: Job had only heard about God before; now he sees our Father face to face, and the distant-deity caricature is gone forever.

lines.t: My ears had heard of you
but now my eyes have seen you.

verses.fhb_text: Therefore I take back my words, and I repent in dust and ashes."
verses.decision_notes: Job's repentance flows from the relational encounter, not from the friends' accusations; "take back my words" renders em'as (despise/reject) in its self-retracting sense.

lines.t: Therefore I take back my words
and I repent in dust and ashes."

verses.fhb_text: After our Father had spoken these words to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite, "My anger burns against you and your two friends, for you have not spoken rightly about me, as my servant Job has.
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: The Father's rebuke — the friends spoke of him as a distant deity; Job reached toward him as Father (FHB-2.9-1).

Renderings:
• "our Father" then pronoun "he said" — established referent, pronoun shift (FHB-9.2-1)
• "My anger burns... My servant Job" — Father self-references in first person, no padding (FHB-1.2-1 v4.3)

Hebrew/Greek: lo dibartem elai nekhonah (you have not spoken to/of me what is right) → preserved
verses.footnote: The friends spoke of God as a distant deity exacting accounts; Job, through all his pain and protest, kept reaching toward our Father — and our Father honored it.

verses.fhb_text: So now take seven bulls and seven rams, go to my servant Job, and offer a burnt offering for yourselves. My servant Job will pray for you, and I will accept his prayer and not deal with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken rightly about me, as my servant Job has."
verses.decision_notes:
Headline: Job intercedes — the suffering righteous one prays for those who wronged him, foreshadowing Christ's intercession.

Renderings:
• "I will accept his prayer" — Father speaks in first person
• "my servant Job" (3x in vv.7-8) — the Father's repeated vindication of Job preserved

verses.fhb_text: So Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as our Father had told them, and our Father accepted Job's prayer.
verses.decision_notes: Narrator voice; YHWH → our Father. The friends obey and are restored only through the intercession of the one they had condemned.

verses.fhb_text: And when Job prayed for his friends, our Father restored his fortunes and gave him twice as much as he had before.
verses.decision_notes: Restoration follows Job's act of intercession; result-first phrasing emphasizes the timing (FHB-9.4-1).

verses.fhb_text: Then all his brothers and sisters and everyone who had known him before came and ate bread with him in his house. They consoled and comforted him over all the trouble our Father had brought upon him, and each one gave him a piece of silver and a gold ring.
verses.decision_notes: Narrator; YHWH → our Father. "a piece of silver" renders qesitah (an archaic weight of silver); footnote category C3_qesitah already used in earlier locked chapters, so no repeat footnote.

verses.fhb_text: Our Father blessed Job's later years more than his earlier ones. He had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, a thousand pairs of oxen, and a thousand female donkeys.
verses.decision_notes: Father as subject of the blessing (FHB-9.4-1); pronoun "He had" shifts to Job as the next clause's subject.

verses.fhb_text: He also had seven sons and three daughters.
He named the first daughter Jemimah, the second Keziah, and the third Keren-happuch.
verses.decision_notes: Daughters named at the source's naming moment; transliterations preserved (no modern equivalents).
verses.footnote: The daughters' names celebrate restored beauty and joy: Jemimah means 'dove,' Keziah means 'cinnamon,' and Keren-happuch means 'horn of eye cosmetics.'

verses.fhb_text: Nowhere in all the land were there women as beautiful as Job's daughters, and their father gave them an inheritance along with their brothers.
verses.decision_notes: Notable detail: daughters receive an inheritance alongside sons — unusual in the ancient world; preserved as written.
verses.footnote: In the ancient world daughters rarely inherited alongside sons — Job's gift to them was an extraordinary act of grace and equality.

verses.fhb_text: After this Job lived one hundred and forty years, and he saw his children and his children's children, four generations.
verses.decision_notes: "children" renders banav / bnei vanav with warm familial language (FHB-5.10-1) rather than clinical "sons/descendants."

verses.fhb_text: And so Job died, old and full of days.
verses.decision_notes: Closing formula; "full of days" preserved as the Hebrew idiom for a life completed in peace.

chapter_notes: Job 42 is the climax of the Father-revelation arc (FHB-2.9-1). Job's confession (vv.1-6) culminates at 42:5 — the move from hearing about God in the friends' distant-deity register to seeing our Father face to face. The Father then rebukes the three friends (vv.7-8) for NOT speaking of him rightly, while vindicating Job who reached toward him as a person; Job intercedes for them, foreshadowing the suffering righteous one praying for his accusers. Restoration (vv.9-17) follows the act of intercession. Speaker grammar applied throughout: narrator → 'our Father'; Job in prayer → direct 'you'; the Father → first-person 'I'/'my servant.' Two footnotes anchor the arc's two spine moments (42:5 revelation, 42:7 rebuke); both categories are new (not in the already-used list). qesitah (v.11) carried no footnote since C3_qesitah is already used.

headings.text: Now My Eyes Have Seen You
